note_data.title: Coordinate geometry
keyPoints: The **equation of a straight line** can be written as y = mx + c where m is the gradient and c is the y-intercept
**Gradient** of a line through points (x₁, y₁) and (x₂, y₂) is m = (y₂ - y₁)/(x₂ - x₁)
**Parallel lines** have equal gradients and **perpendicular lines** have gradients whose product is -1
The **equation of a circle** with centre (a, b) and radius r is (x - a)² + (y - b)² = r²
To find **points of intersection**, solve the equations simultaneously by substitution or elimination
A **tangent to a circle** touches the circle at exactly one point and is perpendicular to the radius at that point
The **distance** between two points (x₁, y₁) and (x₂, y₂) is √[(x₂ - x₁)² + (y₂ - y₁)²]
To find the **centre and radius** of a circle, complete the square for both x and y terms
**Discriminant** determines intersection: Δ > 0 (two points), Δ = 0 (one point/tangent), Δ < 0 (no intersection)

definitions.definition: The measure of steepness of a line, calculated as the change in y divided by the change in x
A straight line that touches a circle at exactly one point
A straight line joining two points on the circumference of a circle
A line that passes through the midpoint of a line segment and is perpendicular to it

howToAnswer.instruction: Calculate the exact value showing all working steps clearly
Prove the given result by working from the given information to reach the stated conclusion
Use given information to work out the answer, showing clear reasoning

modelAnswer.question: A circle has equation x² + y² - 6x + 4y - 12 = 0. Find the centre and radius of the circle.
modelAnswer.answer:
Complete the square: (x² - 6x + 9) + (y² + 4y + 4) - 12 - 9 - 4 = 0
(x - 3)² + (y + 2)² = 25
Centre is (3, -2) and radius is 5
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Complete the square correctly for both x and y terms

commonMistakes: Never write gradient as Δx/Δy — always write as Δy/Δx
Students often forget to change signs when completing the square for circle equations
Writing perpendicular gradient as negative reciprocal instead of stating m₁ × m₂ = -1
Forgetting to take the square root when finding radius from r²
Not showing substitution steps clearly when finding intersection points
examinerTips: Coordinate geometry appears in every Paper 1 and often combines with other topics
Always show substitution working when solving simultaneous equations
Check your gradient calculations by ensuring the fraction is in simplest form
When finding intersections, always verify answers by substituting back into original equations
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: A coordinate plane showing a circle with clearly marked centre, radius, and a tangent line at a point of contact, with intersection points marked where a line meets the circle
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q8 (6 marks) — they asked students to find the equation of a line perpendicular to 3x + 4y = 12 passing through point (2, -1)
This topic appeared in O/N 2022 P2 Q5 (5 marks) — candidates had to find the coordinates of the midpoint and prove that a triangle was right-angled using coordinate geometry
This topic was examined in M/J 2022 P2 Q7 (7 marks) — students needed to find the area of a triangle using coordinates and determine if three points were collinear
This topic featured in O/N 2023 P2 Q6 (4 marks) — they tested finding the distance between two points and using it to determine the radius of a circle

workedExample.question: Find the equation of the line passing through A(1, 3) and B(4, -2). Give your answer in the form ax + by + c = 0. (4 marks)
steps: Step 1: Find the gradient using m = (y₂ - y₁)/(x₂ - x₁) = (-2 - 3)/(4 - 1) = -5/3
Step 2: Use point-slope form y - y₁ = m(x - x₁) with point A(1, 3): y - 3 = -5/3(x - 1)
Step 4: Multiply through by 3 to eliminate fractions: 3y = -5x + 14, rearrange to get 5x + 3y - 14 = 0
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for correct gradient calculation, 1 mark for substituting into point-slope form, 1 mark for correct algebraic manipulation, 1 mark for final answer in required form

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused the gradient of perpendicular lines, incorrectly using m₁ × m₂ = 1 instead of m₁ × m₂ = -1' — always remember perpendicular gradients multiply to give -1
Examiner Report 2022: 'Students frequently made sign errors when substituting negative coordinates into distance and midpoint formulas' — always use brackets when substituting negative values

Examiner Report 2023: 'Candidates often failed to simplify their final equations or left answers with fractions when integers were expected' — always check if your equation can be simplified by multiplying through by the LCM
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must be able to draw a coordinate plane showing two points A(x₁, y₁) and B(x₂, y₂) connected by a line, with the midpoint M clearly marked at ((x₁+x₂)/2, (y₁+y₂)/2). The diagram should include right-angled triangles to illustrate distance calculations, showing the horizontal distance |x₂-x₁| and vertical distance |y₂-y₁|, with the hypotenuse representing the actual distance √[(x₂-x₁)² + (y₂-y₁)²].
note_data.quickRecap: Distance between points: d = √[(x₂-x₁)² + (y₂-y₁)²]. Midpoint formula: M = ((x₁+x₂)/2, (y₁+y₂)/2). Gradient: m = (y₂-y₁)/(x₂-x₁). Line equation: y - y₁ = m(x - x₁). Parallel lines have equal gradients; perpendicular lines have gradients that multiply to -1.
